sections.header: Verses 1–10 — “Babylon Has Fallen! All the Images of Its Gods Lie Shattered”

heb.paragraph: "Fallen, fallen (nāfəlâ) is Babylon! All the images of its gods lie shattered on the ground!" (v.9). A dire vision. The desert by the sea: invaders come from the desert like whirlwinds (v.1). A treacherous traitor betrays; a looter loots (v.2). Elam and Media attack (v.2). Isaiah’s body writhes in pain at the vision; his heart falters, horror overwhelms him (vv.3–4). A watchman: "What do you see?" Two riders approach. Then the announcement: "Babylon has fallen!"

refs.note: Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great! — quoting Isa 21:9 verbatim.
Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great! She has become a dwelling for demons — Revelation’s full development.
Babylon will suddenly fall and be broken — Jeremiah’s parallel oracle.
That very night Belshazzar was slain, and Darius the Mede took over — the narrative fulfilment of the fall.

notes.note: A dire vision. Desert whirlwinds. Elam and Media summoned. The agents of Babylon’s fall named 150 years before the event.
Isaiah’s body writhes; heart falters; horror overwhelms. The physical cost of apocalyptic vision.
The watchman waits. Riders approach. Then: "Fallen, fallen is Babylon!" Rev 14:8 and 18:2 quote this doubled announcement.
"All the images of its gods lie shattered." The theological summary: Babylon’s gods cannot save Babylon. The contest of gods is over.
Calvin reads Isaiah’s physical distress as evidence that true prophecy is costly: the prophet does not announce judgment from a safe distance but participates in its weight.
Calvin: the doubled "fallen, fallen" announces both the historical fall (539 BC) and the eschatological fall (Rev 18). The doubling signals finality: Babylon’s fall is irreversible.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET identifies "the Desert by the Sea" as Babylon — paradoxically located between the desert and the marshes of the lower Euphrates. The geographical description is accurate.
The NET notes the doubled verb (nāfəlâ, nāfəlâ) emphasises certainty and completeness. Revelation’s quotation (14:8; 18:2) preserves the doubling.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt reads the oracle as the second Babylon-oracle (after chs.13–14), confirming the pattern: every major theme in Isaiah is stated and then restated with development.

Oswalt: the "shattered gods" (v.9b) is the oracle’s deepest claim. Babylon’s fall is not merely political but theological: its gods are proven impotent. The idol-trial theme (41:21–24; 44:9–20) begins here.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the doubled "fallen" as canonical: the phrase bridges Isaiah and Revelation. What Isaiah announces, John completes. The canonical Bible reads these as one continuous prophecy.
Childs: the shattered images are the visual proof of monotheism. When Babylon falls, its gods fall with it. Only YHWH remains standing.

hist.context: The doubled "fallen, fallen" is quoted directly in Revelation 14:8 and 18:2 for eschatological Babylon. Isaiah is physically devastated by the vision (vv.3–4), paralleling Daniel’s collapse (Dan 8:27; 10:8–9). The watchman motif (vv.6–9) creates dramatic tension: the audience waits with the sentinel until the riders arrive with news. Elam and Media (v.2) are the specific agents of Babylon’s fall — Cyrus was king of both regions when he conquered Babylon in 539 BC.

heb.paragraph: "Watchman (shōmēr), what is left of the night? Watchman, what is left of the night?" (v.11). Oracle against Dumah (Edom, wordplay on "silence"): the watchman is asked repeatedly about the night. His answer: "Morning is coming, but also the night. If you would ask, then ask; and come back yet again" (v.12). Oracle against Arabia: fugitives in the thickets, bring water for the thirsty, bread for the fugitives (vv.13–15). "Within one year all the splendour of Kedar will come to an end" (v.16).

refs.note: My soul waits for the Lord more than watchmen wait for the morning — the same night-watching motif.
The night is nearly over; the day is almost here — the NT version of the watchman’s question.
The oracle against Edom — the extended judgment on the same nation addressed in the Dumah oracle.
Kedar son of Ishmael — the Arabian tribe’s genealogical origin.

notes.note: The Dumah oracle: "Watchman, what is left of the night?" The answer is ambiguous: morning AND night are coming. Hope is mixed with continued darkness.
Arabia: fugitives in the desert. Bring water and bread. The humanitarian dimension of judgment: even enemies need aid.
Within one year, Kedar’s splendour ends. The desert warriors defeated. God’s sovereignty extends to every tribe.

Calvin reads the ambiguous answer as the human condition between the advents: morning IS coming (Christ has come), but night persists (sin and suffering continue). The watchman’s "come back yet again" is the summons to patient faith.
Calvin notes the command to bring water and bread to fugitives (vv.14–15): even within judgment oracles, mercy operates. The enemy’s refugees deserve aid.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the Dumah/Edom wordplay: D-m-h (silence) rearranges the consonants of E-d-m. The "silence" may refer to the desolation awaiting Edom.

The NET observes the watchman’s answer is deliberately non-committal: "morning is coming, but also the night." The future holds both; the questioner must keep asking. The oracle models honest ambiguity.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt calls the Dumah oracle the most theologically sophisticated two verses in the nations-section: it refuses to offer false comfort while acknowledging genuine hope. Both morning and night are real.
Oswalt reads the Arabia oracle as the section’s most compassionate judgment: the command to aid fugitives (vv.14–15) humanises the enemy. Arabia is judged, but its refugees are served.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the Dumah oracle as a canonical paradigm: the question "how long?" is asked throughout Scripture (Ps 13:1–2; Rev 6:10). The answer is always partial until the final morning.

Childs notes the three short oracles (Babylon, Dumah, Arabia) form a triptych: the great empire falls (v.9), the small nation waits in darkness (v.12), the desert warriors flee (v.17). All are under the same sovereignty.

hist.context: The Dumah oracle (vv.11–12) is the most enigmatic passage in Isaiah. "Dumah" means "silence" — a wordplay on Edom (the consonants are rearranged). The repeated question ("what is left of the night?") and the ambiguous answer ("morning comes, but also night") create a deliberately unresolved tension. The oracle refuses to promise pure dawn; night persists alongside morning. The Arabia oracle (vv.13–17) is more concrete: Kedar’s warriors will be depleted within a year, likely by Assyrian campaigns.

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: 2) are the specific agents of Babylon’s fall — Cyrus was king of both regions when he conquered Babylon in 539 BC…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAt this my body is racked with pain, pangs seize me, like those of a woman in labor; I am staggered by what I hear, I am bewildered by what I see.ESVTherefore my loins are filled with anguish; pangs have seized me, like the pangs of a woman in labor; I am bowed down so that I cannot hear; I am dismayed so that I cannot see.NIVAnd the lookout shouted, \u201cDay after day, my lord, I stand on the watchtower; every night I stay at my post.ESVThen he who saw cried out: \u201cUpon a watchtower I stand, O Lord, continually by day, and at my post I am stationed whole nights.

src.title: The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BC)
src.quote: A clay cylinder in which Cyrus of Persia describes his conquest of Babylon and his policy of returning captive peoples to their homelands — the Persian policy that fulfilled Isaiah 44:28–45:1.
src.note: The cylinder confirms Isaiah’s prophecy of a Persian king who would liberate captives and authorize temple rebuilding, whether the prophecy is dated early (8th c.) or late (6th c.).

src.title: The Sennacherib Prism (c.700 BC)
src.quote: Sennacherib’s own account of his 701 BC campaign states he shut Hezekiah up “like a bird in a cage” in Jerusalem — but conspicuously does NOT claim to have captured the city.
src.note: The silence confirms Isaiah 36–37: Sennacherib besieged but never conquered Jerusalem. His own records inadvertently verify Isaiah’s narrative of divine deliverance.

src.title: The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsaᵃ, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A complete copy of Isaiah found at Qumran, dated c.125 BC. The oldest complete biblical manuscript, it is virtually identical to the Masoretic text 1,000 years later.
src.note: The scroll demonstrates the remarkable textual stability of Isaiah across a millennium. The text the church reads today is essentially the text the Qumran community read.

rec.text: "A dire vision. Desert whirlwinds. Elam and Media summoned. The agents of Babylon’s fall named 150 years before the event."

rows.label: Night and Morning — 21:11–17
rows.text: "Watchman, what of the NIGHT?" "Morning comes, but also NIGHT." Kedar’s splendour ends within a year.

hebtext.note: "Fallen, fallen (nāfəlâ) is Babylon! All the images of its gods lie shattered on the ground!" (v.9). A dire vision. The desert by the sea: invaders come from the desert like whirlwinds (v.1). A treacherous traitor betrays; a looter loots (v.2). Elam and Media attack (v.2). Isaiah’s body writhes in pain at the vision; his heart falters, horror overwhelms him (vv.3–4). A watchman: "What do you see?" Two riders approach. Then the announcement: "Babylon has fallen!"

"Watchman (shōmēr), what is left of the night? Watchman, what is left of the night?" (v.11). Oracle against Dumah (Edom, wordplay on "silence"): the watchman is asked repeatedly about the night. His answer: "Morning is coming, but also the night. If you would ask, then ask; and come back yet again" (v.12). Oracle against Arabia: fugitives in the thickets, bring water for the thirsty, bread for the fugitives (vv.13–15). "Within one year all the splendour of Kedar will come to an end" (v.16).

thread.text: Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great! — quoting Isa 21:9 verbatim.
Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great! She has become a dwelling for demons — Revelation’s full development.
Babylon will suddenly fall and be broken — Jeremiah’s parallel oracle.
That very night Belshazzar was slain, and Darius the Mede took over — the narrative fulfilment of the fall.
My soul waits for the Lord more than watchmen wait for the morning — the same night-watching motif.
The night is nearly over; the day is almost here — the NT version of the watchman’s question.

themes.note: Judgment and Prophecy peak: "Fallen, fallen" (Rev 14:8; 18:2). The watchman’s ambiguous answer. Morning AND night.

coaching.tip: 'Babylon has fallen, has fallen!' (v. 9) — the doubled verb signals completeness and certainty. John quotes this exact phrase in Revelation 14:8 and 18:2, applying it to 'Babylon the Great.' Isaiah's oracle against historical Babylon becomes the template for God's final victory over all oppressive power. The repetition echoes across a thousand years of Scripture.

The watchman motif (vv. 6–12) — 'what is left of the night?' — transforms the prophet into a sentinel. He doesn't create the message; he reports what he sees coming. This self-understanding shapes Ezekiel's call as watchman (Ezekiel 33:1–9) and reappears in Habakkuk 2:1. The prophets understood themselves not as innovators but as observers stationed by God to report the view.
